attrs.alt: Stakeholder Sign-Off RACI matrix for agentic AI feature design gate

content.text: Why Stakeholder Sign-Off Needs a RACI

Design sign-off for an agentic AI feature involves six distinct stakeholder roles, each with different expertise, different authority, and different criteria for saying yes. Without an explicit RACI, sign-off becomes either a bottleneck — everyone waits for a single approver who does not have full context — or a formality — the checklist is ticked by whoever is available without the right authority to make the decision.

The RACI model assigns four roles per decision: Responsible (does the work of gathering evidence and preparing the artefact), Accountable (the single person whose yes or no counts — exactly one per row), Consulted (provides input before the decision), and Informed (notified of the outcome but not involved in making it). The most important rule is that every row has exactly one Accountable. Multiple accountables on a single decision is an anti-pattern that produces accountability gaps — when something goes wrong, everyone assumes someone else had final authority.

For agentic AI features, the six stakeholder roles with sign-off authority are Product Manager, Engineering Lead, Safety Reviewer, Legal and Compliance, Design Lead, and Executive Sponsor. Each has different accountabilities and different criteria for sign-off.
The Six Sign-Off Roles
Product Manager

The Product Manager is accountable for the PRD scope, the approved system prompt baseline, and the risk register. This accountability reflects the PM's responsibility for what the product does and does not do. The PM cannot delegate the scope decision to engineering or design — scope is a product decision that the PM must own.
PRD sign-off criteria:

PRD scope list is final with at least five in-scope items and three out-of-scope items; prototype test results meet quality gates (TCR >= 80%, trust rating >= 3.8); success metrics agreed with the data team.
System prompt accountability:

the PM co-owns the system prompt approval with the Engineering Lead because the prompt is simultaneously a product decision (what the agent does) and an engineering artefact (how it is implemented). Both must sign.
Risk register:

the PM holds accountability for ensuring the risk register is complete and all identified risks have named mitigation owners. This does not mean the PM mitigates the risks — it means they are accountable for ensuring the register is not missing critical risks.
Engineering Lead

The Engineering Lead is accountable for technical feasibility and for the post-launch rollback procedure. This reflects the engineering team's responsibility for the system's behaviour under real load conditions and the team's ability to respond when something goes wrong after launch.
Technical feasibility:

before sign-off, the Eng Lead must confirm that the latency budget is achievable at the target model tier, the token cost estimate is within approved budget at the 50th and 90th percentile, and the context window management strategy is implemented or explicitly de-risked.
Eval suite integration:

the automated evaluation suite must be integrated into the CI/CD pipeline with a deployment gate configured before engineering sign-off. A system prompt that cannot be regression-tested automatically is not ready for engineering build.
Rollback authority:

the Eng Lead has accountable authority for post-launch rollback decisions because they have the technical context to assess whether a production issue warrants immediate rollback versus a hotfix. The Executive Sponsor approves go/no-go for launch; the Eng Lead decides whether to pull back after.
Safety Reviewer

The Safety Reviewer is accountable for the design review safety domain and the safety impact assessment approval. In teams without a dedicated safety role, this accountability falls to the most senior technical person with safety expertise — it cannot be skipped or merged with the PM or Legal roles, which have different expertise.
Safety domain ownership:

the Safety Reviewer is the sole accountable party for whether the harm categories are documented, refusal conditions are specified, and PII handling is designed. The PM can consult but cannot override a safety block.
Eval suite coverage:

the safety sign-off criteria include confirmation that the eval suite covers every harm category identified in the safety impact assessment. An eval suite that does not test for documented harms is not complete.
HITL thresholds:
human-in-the-loop thresholds for high-stakes actions must be agreed in writing before safety sign-off. Verbal agreements about HITL thresholds do not survive team transitions.
Legal and Compliance

The Legal and Compliance role is accountable for the legal sign-off, including data residency, AI disclosure, provider Terms of Service review, and sector-specific regulatory compliance. In many organisations this review takes the longest clock time because legal teams have queues and require complete documentation to begin their review.
Start early:

legal review for an agentic AI feature typically requires two to four weeks. Teams that treat legal as the last gate consistently ship late. File the legal review request when the PRD is at 80% complete, not when it is done.
Data residency specificity:

legal sign-off requires knowing which model API endpoint will be called, which data centre that endpoint routes to, and whether each customer's data residency agreement permits that routing. This is a specific technical fact, not a general legal principle.
ToS version tracking:
model provider Terms of Service change. The legal review must be pinned to a specific ToS version, with a process for re-review if the ToS changes before launch.
Design Lead

The Design Lead is accountable for the UX domain of the design review checklist and for the UX prototype sign-off. This separates UX accountability from product accountability: the PM owns scope, but the Design Lead owns whether the experience of that scope is good enough to ship.
Prototype test authority:

the Design Lead reviews prototype test results and determines whether the quality gates have been met. The PM can request an exception, but the Design Lead must agree. Below 80% TCR is a design block, not a scope negotiation.
Accessibility:

the Design Lead is accountable for confirming that the output format meets accessibility requirements. This is a sign-off criterion, not an aspiration — it must be verified before design sign-off, not after launch.
Executive Sponsor

The Executive Sponsor is accountable for the final launch go/no-go decision. This is the only decision in the entire sign-off process where a single senior executive has explicit authority. All other sign-offs are prerequisites — if any earlier sign-off is missing, the go/no-go meeting should not occur.
Prerequisites:

the Executive Sponsor should not be asked to make a go/no-go decision until all other stakeholder sign-offs are complete. Presenting an incomplete sign-off chain to an executive and asking them to override it is poor process design.
Budget approval:
the executive sign-off must include explicit approval of the model API cost at expected launch volume. Cost surprises at scale are a common cause of features being disabled after launch.
Risk acceptance:
the Executive Sponsor reviews the risk register and formally accepts the residual risks. This acceptance is the organisation's record that the risks were known and consciously accepted before launch.
The RACI Template

Use this RACI template for every agentic AI feature. Fill one row per major decision or artefact. Update the stakeholder-specific criteria sections as the feature evolves — they are living documents, not one-time forms.

# stakeholder-sign-off-raci.md
# ─────────────────────────────
# Fill one row per major decision or artefact.
# Exactly one (A) per row. Update before each design review.

| Decision / Artefact                         | PM  | Eng Lead | Safety | Legal | Design | Exec |
|---------------------------------------------|-----|----------|--------|-------|--------|------|
| PRD final scope and success criteria        |  A  |    C     |   C    |   I   |   C    |  I   |
| Design review checklist (all 6 domains)     |  R  |    R     |   A    |   C   |   A    |  I   |
| Safety impact assessment approval          |  I  |    R     |   A    |   C   |   I    |  I   |
| Legal compliance sign-off                  |  I  |    I     |   C    |   A   |   I    |  I   |
| System prompt baseline (approved hash)     |  A  |    A     |   C    |   I   |   C    |  I   |
| UX prototype test sign-off                 |  R  |    I     |   I    |   I   |   A    |  I   |
| Technical feasibility approval             |  I  |    A     |   I    |   I   |   I    |  I   |
| Risk register and mitigation plans         |  A  |    C     |   C    |   C   |   I    |  C   |
| Launch go/no-go decision                   |  R  |    R     |   R    |   R   |   R    |  A   |
| Post-launch rollback authority             |  C  |    A     |   C    |   I   |   I    |  I   |

## Sign-Off Criteria per Stakeholder

### Product Manager (A: PRD scope, system prompt, risk register)
Criteria before sign-off:
- [ ] PRD scope list final (5 in-scope, 3 out-of-scope minimum), reviewed with eng lead
- [ ] Prototype test complete: task completion rate >= 80%, trust rating >= 3.8
- [ ] Success metrics agreed with data team and documented in PRD
- [ ] MVP feature boundary explicitly separates phase-2 items
- [ ] Risk register reviewed and mitigation owners assigned

### Engineering Lead (A: technical feasibility, post-launch rollback)
Criteria before sign-off:
- [ ] Latency budget achievable at target model + infrastructure tier
- [ ] Token cost per session within approved budget at p50 and p90
- [ ] Context window management strategy implemented or de-risked
- [ ] Tool call failure handling designed and in the implementation plan
- [ ] Eval suite integrated into CI/CD pipeline; deployment gate configured
- [ ] Rollback procedure documented and tested in staging

### Safety Reviewer (A: design review safety domain, safety impact assessment)
Criteria before sign-off:
- [ ] All harm categories for this feature documented and mitigated
- [ ] Human-in-the-loop thresholds set for every high-stakes action
- [ ] Prompt injection attack surface analysed; mitigations in system prompt
- [ ] Refusal conditions and phrasing approved in system prompt spec
- [ ] Safety eval suite covers every harm category identified

### Legal / Compliance (A: legal compliance sign-off)
Criteria before sign-off:
- [ ] Data residency for model API calls confirmed compliant
- [ ] AI disclosure language reviewed and approved
- [ ] Model provider ToS reviewed for applicable commercial restrictions
- [ ] Training data opt-out configured and documented
- [ ] Sector-specific regulation (if applicable) addressed in design

### Design Lead (A: design review UX domain, UX prototype sign-off)
Criteria before sign-off:
- [ ] Conversation flow wireframes reviewed and approved
- [ ] Prototype test results reviewed: TCR, CRR, SVC, UTR all meet targets
- [ ] Error and refusal messages reviewed for tone and plain language
- [ ] Accessibility baseline confirmed: screen reader compatible output format
- [ ] Visual design (if applicable) meets brand and contrast standards

### Executive Sponsor (A: launch go/no-go)
Criteria before sign-off:
- [ ] Budget for model API costs at launch volume approved
- [ ] Risk register reviewed and residual risk accepted
- [ ] All other stakeholders have provided their A sign-off
- [ ] Rollback plan reviewed and understood
- [ ] Launch communication plan approved
Tracking Sign-Off Completion

This script reads a completed RACI markdown and reports whether each stakeholder has completed their criteria sections. Run it in CI or as a pre-meeting check to avoid wasting review time on incomplete documentation:

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# sign_off_tracker.py
# Reads stakeholder-sign-off-raci.md and checks whether all
# stakeholders with Accountable (A) rows have completed their criteria.
# Exits 0 if all criteria met, 1 otherwise.
# Usage: python sign_off_tracker.py stakeholder-sign-off-raci.md

import sys
import re

STAKEHOLDER_SECTIONS = [
    "Product Manager",
    "Engineering Lead",
    "Safety Reviewer",
    "Legal / Compliance",
    "Design Lead",
    "Executive Sponsor",
]

def parse_criteria(path):
    with open(path) as f:
        content = f.read()

    sections = {}
    for role in STAKEHOLDER_SECTIONS:
        pattern = rf"### {re.escape(role)}.*?(?=###|\Z)"
        match = re.search(pattern, content, re.DOTALL)
        if not match:
            sections[role] = {"checked": 0, "unchecked": 0}
            continue
        block = match.group()
        checked = len(re.findall(r"- \[x\]", block, re.I))
        unchecked = len(re.findall(r"- \[ \]", block))
        sections[role] = {"checked": checked, "unchecked": unchecked}

    return sections

def main():
    if len(sys.argv) < 2:
        print("Usage: python sign_off_tracker.py <raci.md>")
        sys.exit(2)

    sections = parse_criteria(sys.argv[1])
    any_incomplete = False

    print(f"\nStakeholder Sign-Off Status\n" + "-" * 40)
    for role, stats in sections.items():
        total = stats["checked"] + stats["unchecked"]
        pct = int(100 * stats["checked"] / total) if total else 0
        status = "READY" if stats["unchecked"] == 0 and total > 0 else "PENDING"
        icon = "[OK]" if status == "READY" else "[--]"
        print(f"  {icon} {role}: {stats['checked']}/{total} criteria ({pct}%)  [{status}]")
        if status != "READY":
            any_incomplete = True

    print()
    if any_incomplete:
        print("NOT READY FOR LAUNCH — pending stakeholder sign-offs remain.")
        sys.exit(1)
    else:
        print("ALL SIGN-OFFS COMPLETE — gate open for launch.")
        sys.exit(0)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Common RACI Failures
Multiple Accountables on one row:
produces deadlock when the two accountables disagree and no one has authority to break the tie. Choose one and make the other Consulted.
Safety merged with Legal:
safety expertise (harm analysis, prompt injection, HITL) is different from legal expertise (data residency, ToS, regulation). Merging them into one role produces weak coverage of both.
Executive Sponsor as Accountable for too many rows:
executive sign-off is valuable precisely because it is rare. Using the executive as the accountable for technical and UX decisions devalues the launch gate and creates a bottleneck.
PM as sole Accountable for system prompt:

the system prompt is both a product decision and a technical artefact. Engineering must co-own it because engineers are the ones who can judge whether the prompt will behave as specified under adversarial inputs.
Informed parties not actually informed:

the Informed column is often treated as 'not relevant to this person.' If a stakeholder is marked I, they must receive notification of the decision outcome in writing. Skipping this creates surprises at the launch gate.
Sign-off criteria left blank:
a RACI without explicit sign-off criteria per stakeholder is a placeholder, not a gate. Each Accountable must have at least three measurable criteria before their sign-off counts.
Sign-Off Timeline and Sequencing
The six sign-off streams are not fully sequential — some can run in parallel. The following sequence minimises total elapsed time:
PRD reaches 80% complete: file legal review request immediately. Legal has the longest queue.
Complete rapid prototype testing: Design Lead reviews results within 48 hours of last session.
Run design review meeting: all six stakeholders present. Identify RED and AMBER items.
Safety impact assessment: Safety Reviewer completes within one week of design review.
Technical feasibility sign-off: Eng Lead signs off after safety assessment is complete (safety decisions affect technical requirements).
Legal sign-off: arrives when legal review is complete. Usually determines the critical path.
PM signs off: after all domain sign-offs are confirmed. PRD is frozen at this point.
Executive go/no-go: scheduled only after all other sign-offs are complete. Keep this meeting under 30 minutes — the preparation was done in the preceding steps.

The critical path is almost always Legal. Plan for this by filing the legal review request at least four weeks before the target launch date, and by treating the Legal sign-off as the pacing constraint for the entire design phase.
Handling Sign-Off Blocks and Escalations

A structured escalation matrix prevents sign-off blocks from stalling indefinitely. Every block has a resolution path and a time limit. Without an explicit escalation process, blocks tend to persist until the launch deadline forces a rushed decision — precisely the scenario that produces the worst outcomes for agentic AI features.

The key principle is that safety and legal blocks are hard blocks: no exception process exists and no executive can override them. All other blocks are soft blocks that the Executive Sponsor can grant a time-bound exception for, with a written remediation plan. Treating safety and legal as hard blocks is not bureaucratic rigidity — it is a clear signal to the organisation that these domains exist to prevent real harm, not to create paperwork.

# escalation-matrix.md
# When a stakeholder cannot reach sign-off by the scheduled date,
# use this matrix to determine the escalation path.

## Escalation Levels

Level 1 — Direct escalation (try first, resolves 70% of blocks)
  Actor  : PM reaches out directly to the blocked stakeholder
  Method : Written summary of the blocking issue + proposed resolution + deadline
  SLA    : Stakeholder responds within 2 business days
  Trigger: Sign-off overdue by more than 3 business days

Level 2 — Facilitated resolution (for disagreements between stakeholders)
  Actor  : Engineering Lead or PM facilitates a 30-minute sync
  Attendees: Blocked stakeholder + the stakeholder whose output they depend on
  Method : Present the specific blocking item, propose two alternative resolutions,
           agree on one in the meeting
  SLA    : Meeting within 2 business days of Level 1 failure
  Trigger: Level 1 produced no resolution within 2 days

Level 3 — Executive escalation (for unresolvable disagreements)
  Actor  : Executive Sponsor
  Method : Written escalation report: nature of disagreement, options, recommendation
  SLA    : Decision from Exec within 1 business day of receiving the report
  Trigger: Level 2 produced no resolution; or the block impacts the launch date by > 1 week

## Per-Stakeholder Escalation Contacts

  PM           -> Engineering Director
  Eng Lead     -> VP Engineering
  Safety       -> Chief Safety Officer or equivalent
  Legal        -> General Counsel
  Design Lead  -> VP Product or VP Design
  Exec Sponsor -> Board or COO (for critical launches only)

## Block Classification

  RED HARD BLOCK : safety or legal sign-off missing. Launch cannot proceed under any
                   circumstance. No exception process exists. Resolve or descope.

  RED SOFT BLOCK : non-safety, non-legal sign-off missing. Executive Sponsor may grant
                   a time-bound exception (max 72 hours post-launch) with a written
                   remediation plan. Requires Exec sign-off on the exception itself.

  AMBER ITEM     : sign-off obtained but a condition remains. The condition must be
                   resolved before the UAT gate (not the design gate). Assign owner
                   and deadline in the RACI document.
Integrating Sign-Off with the Risk Register

The sign-off process and the risk register are tightly coupled. Every item that a stakeholder marks as AMBER during their sign-off should appear as a risk entry in the register with a named owner, a mitigation action, and a resolution deadline. This creates a single document where open items are tracked regardless of which domain they originate in.
AMBER to risk mapping:

when the Eng Lead defers context window management to an AMBER item, that item becomes a risk entry: 'long conversation sessions may produce incoherent responses due to context truncation.' The mitigation, owner, and target resolution date are the same as in the sign-off document.
Risk closure triggers sign-off update:
when a risk is resolved, the corresponding AMBER item in the sign-off RACI is updated to GREEN. The sign-off tracker script should be re-run after each risk closure to confirm the gate status.
Residual risk acceptance:

some risks will be accepted rather than mitigated — this is explicitly the Executive Sponsor's decision at the go/no-go gate. The risk register must show which risks are accepted (not merely unresolved) to distinguish between conscious acceptance and oversight.
Post-launch risk review:

schedule a risk register review at 30 days post-launch. Production observations often reveal that mitigations were less effective than expected, or that new risks appeared that were not in the design-phase register.
Sign-Off Versioning and Audit Trail

Every time the PRD scope changes, the system prompt changes, or a domain sign-off is granted, the change must be recorded with a timestamp and the identity of the accountable approver. This audit trail serves two purposes: it enables rollback to a previously approved state if a change introduces a regression, and it provides evidence of due diligence if the feature is later scrutinised by regulators or by legal discovery.
Prompt hash as version key:

every approved system prompt version has a hash recorded in the sign-off document. If the prompt changes after sign-off, the new hash is recorded and the sign-off must be re-granted for the affected domains.
Sign-off expiry:

a design sign-off that is more than 60 days old must be re-reviewed before a launch that was delayed beyond that window. Model behaviour changes with provider updates; a 60-day-old safety sign-off may no longer reflect the model's current behaviour.
Immutable records:

sign-off records should be stored in a system that prevents retroactive modification — a git-tracked document, a ticketing system with an audit log, or a signed PDF. Editable shared documents are not adequate audit records.
